data.title: August Jobs Report: A Late Summer Chill As Job Growth Stalls
data.description:
It’s not just temperatures that are cooling down as summer nears its end. The labor market is losing steam, and eyebrows are being raised. Today’s release was sure to be a closely examined report, and the data warrants the attention.

Ahead of the A.M. release, U.S equities were seeking direction following a weak start to the September trading month. On Tuesday, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY), and Nasdaq (NDX) all finished lower following a yield spike in government bonds across the Atlantic, as well as due to rising inflation concerns. Equities then seesawed higher on Wednesday, led by gains from Alphabet (GOOG), Apple (AAPL), and Dell (DELL).

In the pre-market trading hours just before the 8:30 AM Friday release, equities began the day higher but mostly flat before rising following a much weaker than expected job release.. In the bond market, yields on the 10-year Treasury (US10Y) stabilized around the 4.15% mark after volatility earlier in the week.

The significance of today’s report was not just in the readout, which landed well below expectations, but also because it was the first report since the firing of the former agency head and the first since Federal Reserve (“Fed”) Chairman Jerome Powell’s comments regarding the state of the labor market. Today’s report is likely to keep the Fed on track to initiate at least a quarter point cut at its next meeting. Here’s what else to know about the August jobs report.

What Sectors Added Jobs In August

Just 22,000 jobs were added in August, well below the expected figure in the mid 70,000 range. Additionally, data in June was revised down into negative territory from 14,000 to a -13,000. This more than offset the upward revision to July to 79,000.

Driving the gains in employment were just two sectors, health care and social assistance. The former added 31,000 jobs during the month. While positive, this still represented a slower pace of growth than the average monthly gain of 42,000 jobs over the last twelve months. These gains were supplemented by growth of 16,000 in social assistance.

Almost every other sector reported net declines during the month. The federal government continued to shed jobs, reporting a decline of 15,000, while wholesale trade reported declines of 12,000.

Gains and losses were fairly muted elsewhere. This could be viewed as a positive in light of the negative disparity elsewhere.

August Unemployment And Wage Growth

Over the past several months, the unemployment rate has tracked in a narrow range despite the weakening in hiring rates. This has been a positive that has provided somewhat of an anchor to the health of the labor economy. It also signals that while employers are reluctant to hire new workers, they are almost equally reluctant to let go of existing workers.

In August, the unemployment rate ticked up slightly to 4.3% from 4.2% in the month prior, in-line with expectations. The increase followed little change in the participation rate. In August, the rate held at 62.3%.

While the increase is worth nothing, I don't view it as concerning.

Fed Policy Implications

Today’s data is likely to further reinforce the near-certain expectation that the Fed will move to ease monetary policy at its upcoming September meeting. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, markets are pricing in a near-certain probability of a quarter-point rate cut later this month, with roughly even odds of another cut in October.

These expectations reflect not just cooling labor market data, but also the Fed’s heightened sensitivity to the risk that a modest slowdown in hiring could quickly translate into broader job losses.

At the annual symposium at Jackson Hole, comments made by current and former Fed officials support this notion. Most importantly, Powell has acknowledged that while inflation remains above target, rising risks of the labor market justifies more attention to the labor dynamics. Former Fed advisers have similarly warned that once layoffs accelerate, the Fed may be forced to respond more aggressively.

On top of all of this is the current political environment for the Fed. President Trump’s nominee, Stephen Miran, recently testified before the Senate Banking Committee that he would act independently if confirmed as a Fed governor. While his comments sought to reassure lawmakers of the Fed’s autonomy, his nomination highlights the ongoing tensions between central bank independence and the administration’s demands for a looser policy stance.

As it stands today, the path ahead is relatively clear, in my view. The Fed is preparing to cut, with markets closely watching whether September marks the start of a broader easing cycle.

Final Thoughts On The August Jobs Report

Beyond the fireworks stemming from the firing of the former agency head, the primary message in the August jobs release is that the labor economy is cooling down, and it's cooling down rather rapidly. Job growth landed well below expectations, and this was paired by further negative revisions in the prior months data. Unemployment, meanwhile, remained stable despite the anemic job growth. This suggests employers are continuing to resist widespread layoffs on an overall basis, at least for the time being.

That said, as recent data has shown, companies are largely holding off on adding new workers, reflecting caution in the face of tariffs, policy uncertainty, and still-elevated borrowing costs. This dynamic consisting of slow hiring/low firing creates a tricky balancing scale where even a modest increase in layoffs could quickly tip the market into net job losses.

One could perhaps be forgiven if pointing to recent data elsewhere, which also hasn’t been promising. The ADP payroll report, for example, released yesterday, came in below expectations. This shows weakening private-sector hiring momentum. Meanwhile, survey data shows that more employers plan to slow hiring in the second half of 2025 compared to last year. In my view, younger workers and lower-income households, who rely most on job switches for upward mobility, are particularly exposed in this environment.

As for the Fed, the report provides additional confirmation that the labor market is losing momentum. As such, the Fed is likely to move ahead with a quarter-point rate cut at its September meeting.

data.title: IYW: Technology And The P/E Barrier (Downgrade)
data.description:
It is not the first time I have analyzed iShares® U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), and in my last coverage, I had the claim of trying to anticipate the bottom by reasoning in a contrarian perspective. The BUY rating came after a 10% dump, a few days after the introduction of tariffs. Let me say that we were not all in agreement.

But it is normal; there was too much uncertainty.

But the point of supporting a BUY rating on that occasion was to try to make room for technicalities, and let slip away what I like to call “news bulletin phobia.” Well, I say this because now the sentiment is practically the opposite, which is why I think the right rating returns to being HOLD.

And now I will explain why.

What you need to know about IYW

Here I open a small TLTR list, with the characteristics that, in my opinion, are absolutely necessary to know about this ETF:

  • It is a passive ETF that replicates the performance of the Russell 1000 Technology RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index with a representative sampling technique; not surprisingly, it has a low turnover, 7%.

  • And why do I say that? Because despite this structure, participating in IYW costs 0.39%, even if spreads are tight.

  • Also, the capping system is very simple, quarterly rebalancing, and 22.5% max per single issuer; 45% max for the sum of issuers > 4.5% each.

  • Its main peers are XLK and VGT, and what differentiates them is basically the benchmark. Although all three are concentrated on the tech sector, the index to which they are anchored is different (respectively, Technology Select Sector Index and MSCI US IMI/Information Technology 25/50 Index); therefore, so is their degree of representativeness.

Holding distribution:

Result? 141 stocks, but well over 60% is concentrated in the top 10 holdings and specifically in two macro themes, software & services (40.96%) and semiconductors (31.58%).

Evidence that, as widely discussed here, shows how two sectors are practically 70% of the tech market, and how they are closely linked to each other, because of AI (the catalyst of modern EPS growth). And it is an issue that I care about, and that particularly worries me, because in fact there is a concentration risk in my opinion particularly relevant today in tech. I invite you to read this insight.

Clear evidence

Now, replicating the method used to identify the floor price in the previous update, I wonder, what could be a target price worth taking a position on? Simply put, if it approaches, it is a BUY rating, otherwise not.

Some key data:

  • Today’s P/E: 40.41.

  • The median P/E is 31x

  • EPS growth today is 19.6% with a P of $180, which means that the forward P/E is 33.8x.

How can we reason? I make a forcing (as in the last update) and take as equilibrium forward P/E the median P/E (a test I like to do to orient myself on stocks with momentum above grade A). And by doing so, I easily identify a balance price that reflects prospective growth, and a valuation historically proven sustainable.

Result? The market prices more than the expected growth. The floor therefore shifts to $165, which is 7%-8% below current quotations. This is why, in my opinion, tech enters the Hold rating range.

Why not SELL? (risk section)

Is it too technical/cynical an approach? I don’t think so.

It would have been if this had opened the doors to a SELL rating. A rating that, in my opinion, has absolutely no reason to exist on technology ETFs like IYW. We are talking about a sector with expected EPS equal to 19.6%, with momentum grade A and SA rating BUY. The statistics are definitely against any sale.

I think tech is a segment to overweight in a portfolio, naturally, the way I work, at the right prices, and with a long-term perspective.

The risk? Of course, a sector at extreme P/Es like now is also statistically the most susceptible to external shocks. Simply put, it is the one that suffered most when Trump introduced tariffs, precisely because it was the most overvalued (higher-than-expected EPS are discounted in valuations).

Conclusion

I tend to maintain a methodical approach when it comes to core ETFs for the long-term portfolio (those who follow me know this), and on IYW I prefer to optimize the entry points in areas where the relative valuation is at least in balance. Today it is in disequilibrium, and a buy would unbalance the risk and the average entry price toward levels not justified by the expected growth, which is why I move from BUY to HOLD.

data.title: National Beverage: Hold For Steady Returns Amid Sparkling Water Growth
data.description:
Introduction

National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ) is an American beverage company best known for its LaCroix Sparkling Water brands. Established in 1985 by businessman Nick Caporella, it was created by a succession of acquisitions of brands like Shasta and Faygo, two old soda brands that still exist in its portfolio to date. Headquartered in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, the company gradually switched its focus to healthier beverages, becoming one of the early players in the fast-growing sparkling water market.

Today, National Beverage has a market capitalization of around $3.8 billion and is quoted on the NASDAQ exchange. The company has minimal debt and holds over $300 million in cash and regularly maintains some of the best margins in the soft drink space. This is an interesting time to study National Beverage because consumer preferences are shifting. Consumption is moving away from sugary sodas due to obesity concerns toward calorie-free and functional beverages.

In the meantime, competition in this arena is rising, with multinational corporations such as Coca-Cola, PepsiCo, and Nestlé putting tremendous effort into gaining market share. In such a scenario, National Beverage offers a great case study of a niche winner with profitability and financial strength with the issue of preserving its leadership in a very competitive category.

Company’s Operations and Market Situation

National Beverage classifies its portfolio of products into two categories. Its "Power+" brands comprise LaCroix sparkling water, Clear Fruit flavored water products, Rip It high-caffeine energy drinks, and Everfresh juices. These are positioned for the health-minded consumer looking for low-calorie or functional products, with LaCroix comprising the vast majority of sales. Complementing these are its core soda brands, Shasta and Faygo, with which it still supports its classic carbonated soft drink customer base. In recent years, however, LaCroix has become National's growth engine, contributing an estimated four-fifths of all revenue.

Operationally, National has twenty-four manufacturing facilities it owns and two on lease, all these being in the United States of America. It has that structure to give National maximum control over manufacturing and distribution. It adopts a hybrid go-to-market model of warehouse shipments and direct-store delivery to mass merchandise stores to reach its end consumers. Its channel partners are chains of national supermarket chains, mass merchandise stores, convenience stores, and regional distributors. Although it operates with a moderate risk of having access to Canada, it is otherwise a largely domestic company with huge potential for growth in the future with international forays should management wish to do that. Strategic initiatives over recent years have focused on innovation, advertising, and investments in operational capital.

During fiscal 2025, National launched new LaCroix products such as Sunshine, Cherry Lime, and Blackberry Cucumber that reversed several quarters of downward-spiraling case shipments. Its programs of marketing have expanded to include a summer-season branded bus tour and sponsorship arrangements with professional sports teams across soccer, the WNBA, and the professional National Hockey League. To fund these initiatives, capital investments peaked at about $30 million in fiscal 2024 and should remain at similar figures for fiscal 2025, implying investments in refurbishment of facilities and expansion of capacity. National has never made massive-scale acquisitions or mergers but rather opted for organic growth of its brands and judicious spending of capital.

The larger non-carbonated beverage market offers opportunity and threat in equal part. The seltzer water category has become a multibillion-dollar market and continues to grow by double-digit percentages throughout the next decade globally. Back in the United States, though, it is becoming increasingly competitive, with PepsiCo's Bubly, Coca-Cola's AHA, and Nestlé's San Pellegrino all going head-to-head with LaCroix outright. In spite of that, though, that larger wellness-themed consumption theme is most definitely on National's side of the ledger, with that battle for market share being intense but category growth throughout the long haul looking positive for National to still be in the mix.

Financial Overview

Stabilizing profit margins were demonstrated by National Beverage, while revenue growth has been disappointing. The revenue of National Beverage for its 2024 fiscal year that ended Apr 27, 2024, was $1.19 billion, with year-over-year increases of 1.6 percent. Its gross margin was 36 percent, from 33.8 percent last year, due to cost savings from reduced costs for packaging programs and select price programs. Operating income was $218.6 million, and net income was $176.7 million, or $1.89 a share, from $1.64 last year.

During its 2025 fiscal year that closed May 3, 2025, record performance was realized with flat sales growth. Net revenue was $1.20 billion, operating income was $235.5 million, and net income was $186.8 million, or $1.99 per share for the quarter. Gross margin widened once again to 37 percent to show the ability to hold and actually regain profitability with flat volumes.

Quarterly trends provide more detail. In first-half 2025 financials, revenue decreased modestly while case volumes fell 2 to 3 percent, but stronger pricing made up part of the impact. By the fourth quarter, however, new LaCroix products and improved ad programs created a 5.5 percent revenue increase and almost a 9 percent operating income gain. The spike showed management's capabilities for creating demand when supported with innovation and brand initiatives.

The balance sheet remains a significant strength. National Beverage ended FY 2025 with $327 million in cash and equivalents and no debt. Operating annual cash flow remains strong at approximately $170 million, and liquidity ratios are robust, with current assets over triple current liabilities. The management has not issued explicit numerical EBITDA for FY 2026 but is positive that innovation of products and brand loyalty will sustain growth further. The financial performance of the company in the past has demonstrated modest but stable revenue growth accompanied by strong margins indicative of disciplined management and conservative capital structure.

Valuation

At a price of between $41 and $42, National Beverage has a trailing price-to-earnings multiple of between 20 and 21 times and a consensus estimate of about 20 times forward with respect to anticipation of analyst targets. It has an enterprise value-to-EBITDA multiple of about 14 times and a price-to-sales multiple of 3.2. It trades on a price-to-free-cash-flow of about 21 times.

Compared to its peer set, the stock is fairly valued for the most part. Monster has significantly higher multiples, with a P/E of approximately 39 and an EV/EBITDA of 26, while Coca-Cola and Keurig Dr Pepper are in the 20 to 25 times-earnings bracket. A newer functional drink peer, Celsius Holdings, has premium multiples because of its explosive growth curve. By that same logic, National falls somewhere in between with its high-margin strength but low growth. FIZZ valuation multiples over the last five years fluctuated between teens and low 20s on an earnings basis. Current levels are right in the center of that history band, indicating that investors consider it to be a stable cash generator and not a growth disruptor.

Catalysts

Looking ahead, a number of factors can be near-term catalysts for National Beverage. Further LaCroix innovation infusing continued success can help jumpstart volume growth and further fortify relationships with large retail partners. Wider marketing tie-ins and advertising campaigns should increase visibility and end-user interest, potentially driving increased sales. Gradual geographic expansion, especially in Canada and select overseas markets, can diversify revenue sources. Operating leverage from better facility utilization may offer further margin expansion, and with a cash-flush balance sheet, shareholder returns via share buybacks or special dividends remain a strong management tool to help support the stock.

Risks

There are also several risks for National Beverage investors to think hard about. It faces growing competition in its sparkling water market with giant multinationals capable of out-marketing it and securing better shelf space. Volumes down that were experienced over recent years just demonstrate how difficult it is to hold growth in a developed American market. Over-reliance on LaCroix being its lead product creates concentration risk, and its geographic coverage being fairly low creates vulnerability that is nearly entirely due to trends amongst U.S. drinkers. Increases in input costs, particularly in packaging and in aluminum, and potential changes in regulations like new taxes on drinks are others. And last but not least, valuation risk cannot be eliminated, with shares already at levels that are in historic norms, and thus leaving no cushion should financials be a disappointment.

Conclusion and Rating

National Beverage Corp. is a cash-generating, minimal-debt firm that has one of the most profitable of all drink businesses' profiles. Its category-leading household name of sparkling water, LaCroix, remains its strongest asset, with disciplined financial management delivering stability across cycles. Record profitability and robust demand performances in fiscal 2025 were assisted with innovation and advertising support. But growth prospects for the company are kept tame with volume pressure and growing rivalry set to limit upside. Dependence of business on a single brand and predominantly domestic footprint once again sums up risks. Valuation comparisons place the stock at parity with its all-time record and peer group, neither discount nor premium being justified for it. These considerations aside, however, our recommendation remains hold. National Beverage is a stable consumer staple play that would suit risk-averse investors looking for balance-sheet strength and shareholder-friendliness. It still lacks a strong enough trigger, such as sustained volume growth or significant overseas expansion, to justify its risk premium over higher-growth plays elsewhere in the space, however.

Rating: HOLD

data.title: 3 REITs That Pay Me $2,000 Each Month
data.description:
One of my followers here on Seeking Alpha recently asked me:

"What are my biggest investments for passive income?"

I found this question interesting because most investors evaluate the size of their investments based on their value rather than the income they generate.

Some of my most significant investments in terms of their value offer a relatively low yield and don't generate much passive income.

In what follows, I will highlight three of my largest investments in terms of the passive income they generate for me. All three are REIT (VNQ) or REIT-adjacent businesses, and they're currently earning me about $2,000 of monthly passive income.

Patria Investments Limited (PAX)

Patria Investments Limited (PAX) takes the top of the list, earning me more than $1,000 per month at the current dividend rate.

Why invest so much in it?

For those of you who are not familiar with Patria, it's essentially the "Blackstone of Latin America." They used to closely collaborate together, and at one point, Blackstone Inc. (BX) even owned a stake in Patria and offered to buy it out entirely. But Patria's management, which owns about 60% of the company, decided to remain independent, believing that they could create a lot more value themselves.

Today, Patria is the leading alternative asset manager in the region, managing nearly $50 billion worth of private equity, real estate, infrastructure, credit, and other alternative investments in exchange for fee income.

I'm particularly bullish on Patria and invest heavily in it because I think that the company offers the potential for significant passive income over the long run.

At first glance, it may not jump out to you as a particularly special dividend stock, offering just a 4.6% dividend yield.

But what you need to consider here is that the company has intentionally kept its dividend rate at a low level in recent years in order to pay off the debt that it took to close a few M&A deals. Its current payout ratio is set at a low level of 47%, down from about 85% when it went public.

Now, this deleveraging is mostly complete, with the company's debt-to-FRE dropping below 1x, even as its cash flow continues to grow at a rapid pace, and therefore, we expect the company to soon change its dividend policy.

This is especially likely because the lack of dividend growth has clearly depressed the company's market sentiment in recent years and hurt its valuation. Its share price has performed poorly in comparison to its close peers that are returning a larger portion of their cash flow to investors and steadily growing their distribution.

As a result, it now trades at a steep discount:

Patria Investments Close Peers
P/FRE 10x 24x

This discount is especially striking when you consider that Patria is growing just as fast as most of its close peers, has lower leverage, and enjoys greater barriers to entry in its local markets.

The company is guided to grow its FRE per share by 15% annually in the coming years:

Yet, the payout ratio is already low, the deleveraging is now complete, and the management has certainly become aware of the fact that the lack of dividend growth is depressing their market sentiment. Since they're the biggest shareholders themselves, this is not in their best interest, so I expect them to instate a new dividend policy soon, possibly at their next Investor Day in December.

If they returned to a variable policy of paying out 85% of their FRE, the dividend could nearly double, resulting in a very high yield. I do not expect that, but even a bump to just 70% would result in a much higher yield.

This could result in significant upside in the stock as well as income investors rush to buy it for its higher dividend yield.

I'm positioning myself ahead of the crowd.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE)

Alexandria Real Estate is my second-largest investment in terms of the passive income it generates for me, at about $600 per month.

The leading life science REIT has historically traded at a very low dividend yield during most times and a high valuation during most times due to its strong growth prospects and exceptional long-term track record.

But its share price has crashed in recent years, even as it kept hiking its dividend, and as a result, it is now offering a 6.7% dividend yield:

The reason why it's so cheap today is that the life science property sector is severely oversupplied, and the market fears that this will cause Alexandria to suffer declining cash flow, which could eventually also put its dividend at risk.

I agree that the oversupply is significant and will hurt the company in the near term.

However, I think that the extent of the damage will be much more limited because Alexandria owns the best of the best assets in this sector, and it enjoys eight-year-long leases on average with 3% annual rent escalations.

The long leases should protect it during this challenging period, and even as leases expire, most tenants are likely to remain in these assets since Alexandria owns the best properties, typically in more supply-constrained mega-campus medical clusters. Moving is expensive and impractical, and it would mean downgrading to a worse property for Alexandria's tenants.

The new construction starts have now dropped to a historic low, even as the demand for space is still expected to grow significantly over time, as the population is aging rapidly, and 90%-plus of the 10,000 known deaths still don't have a proper treatment.

So I think that the oversupply will only cause modest pain, resulting in flat cash flow for a few years, which will then be followed by an acceleration in 2027 as the demand catches up to the supply.

As this acceleration happens, I expect Alexandria to reprice closer to the historic norm, which could result in 50%-plus upside.

While you wait, you earn a 6.7% dividend that should be sustainable. The payout ratio is low at 60%, and the REIT has one of the best balance sheets in the entire sector, enjoying a BBB+ credit rating and 12-year-long debt maturities.

Big Yellow Group Plc (OTCPK:BYLOF)

My third-largest is Big Yellow Group, at about $500 per month.

Its story has similarities with that of Alexandria.

It has been massively successful over the long run, earning its investors 15.8% annual total returns on average ever since it went public up until the recent REIT bear market:

As a result, the market has typically rewarded it with a high valuation, and it would trade at a low yield.

But today, the self-storage property sector is facing oversupply, which is slowing down its growth, and the market has punished it with a historically low valuation. Even then, the REIT has kept hiking its dividend, and as a result, it's now offering a historically high dividend yield of 5%.

This may not seem high to you, but keep in mind that the REIT has very low leverage with a 14% LTV, and it has historically grown its cash flow at a very rapid pace of 12% per year on average.

The oversupply is now slowing this down, but once again, I think that this will prove to be just temporary.

What happened is that the pandemic initially led to a boom in new demand as people needed to make space for a home office, others were moving around a lot, and older generations were passing away and leaving a lot of stuff behind.

This surge in demand was then met with a surge in new construction activity as developers sought to capitalize on this demand.

But then the world went back to normal, and the demand cooled down right as the supply started hitting the market. I think that it will take another year or so for the supply to get absorbed, and then the growth will accelerate.

This could unlock substantial upside, and while you wait, you earn the 5% yield, which equates to about $500 per month in my case.

The company has historically grown its dividend by about 10% per year, so that passive income could reach much higher levels in the years ahead.

Closing Note

Keeping score of the portfolio value is nice, but ultimately, what matters most to me is how much the portfolio is generating in passive income.

That's why at High Yield Landlord, we focus on income first, growth second.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Duluth Holdings Q2 Review: Succeeding Turnaround Supports More Upside
data.description:
Shares of Duluth Holdings (DLTH) soared over 50% on Thursday after it reported better-than-expected results, suggesting the company may be gaining traction in its turnaround efforts. Still, even with this rally, it has been a difficult year for the company, with shares down 11% from a year ago. This is consistent with a long period of underperformance, as shares are down about 70% from five years ago. Duluth has struggled with becoming increasingly promotional to support sales, eroding margins, and management is actively trying to correct these issues to restore margins and profitability.

Shift from excess promotional activity appears to be working

This turnaround does appear to be taking hold to an extent. In the company’s fiscal second quarter, Duluth Holdings earned $0.03, turning a surprise profit and beating estimates by $0.15, even as revenue declined by 7% from last year to $132 million. Obviously, with sales falling, the company is not yet gaining traction among consumers as a whole, but it is encouraging to see the company return to profitability even with slower sales. That is because the company is backing away from unprofitable sales as it tries to maximize the value of its franchise.

In particular, management is focusing on reducing deep discounts. We are seeing signs this strategy is working; the average revenue per unit in men’s bottoms rose by 10% from last year, for example. That is why the company was able to meaningfully expand margins even as sales were $10 million lower than a year ago. Even with lower revenue, adjusted EBITDA grew by nearly 15% to $12 million. This speaks to how much promotions were eating away at its business. I expect the company to exit this transition with a lower sales base but a more profitable one.

Indeed, you can see below that the previous strategy of using promotional activity to support sales was simply not working, necessitating a change in strategies. Like most retailers, Duluth saw a surge in 2021 post-COVID as consumers used their excess savings towards “revenge spending,” though this quickly eroded. More notably, though, you can see that from 2019 to 2024, sales increased by $11 million; however, EBITDA declined by $37 million to just $15 million. The company basically eroded all of its margin in order to sustain sales, which is not a sustainable strategy. This is why the stock has been such a large underperformer over several years.

Duluth will be a smaller but more profitable business

Frankly, the magnitude of the turn this quarter was impressive. Gross margins were 54.7%, up 150bps from last year. Additionally, SG&A was down $5 million from last year to $69 million as it focused on streamlining operating costs and sizing its workforce to better match its sales level. Still, it does continue to invest in its brand; advertising was 8.9% of sales, up from 8.3% a year ago. Overall, Duluth had a 9.1% EBITDA margin, up 170bps from last year.

Now, reducing promotional activity does come with costs, namely lower sales. For example, direct channel sales declined by 18% given lower promotional activity. Offsetting this, store sales were up 5%, due to new locations. Ultimately, there is a balance between shifting promotional policy too aggressively and alienating its core customer base vs moving too slowly and keeping margins unacceptably low. For now, Duluth appears to be managing this balance fairly well, but in a turnaround of this magnitude, I expect there will be bumps along the way.

Ultimately, Duluth is a niche company. It focuses on selling jeans, and other casual workwear for men and women who lead a “hands-on lifestyle.” It is more function- and comfort-focused than more fashion-oriented brands like PVH (PVH) or Levi’s (LEVI). This is not a fast-growing niche, and that is why maximizing the value of its core sales and customer base is so critical.

Importantly, management appears to be recognizing its future is that of a $500-$600 million sales company, not a $700 million sales company. For instance, it aims to cut its SKUs by 20% over the next year. Duluth will not be everything to everyone; rather, it is simplifying its product offerings to maximize margins and still reach the vast majority of its target market. Alongside that, inventory was down 12% from last year to $148 million. It expects inventory to end the year down double digits relative to last year. Even as it cut inventory, Duluth’s store in-stock levels were up 200bps. By reducing product breadth, Duluth is not only able to carry less inventory; it is able to carry more of the right inventory. I would note that 22% of its inventory is in clearance, which is a bit higher than I like to see (I prefer 10-15%), which may weigh on Q3 margins.

Duluth is managing tariffs and macro uncertainty well

Now, there cannot be a discussion of an apparel stock without discussing tariffs. The company had a negligible tariff margin impact in Q2, which will rise in H2. This is consistent with most companies in the sector, as they had inventory on hand ahead of tariffs, and most countries had a 10% tariff in place during Q2. Duluth sees a $15 million tariff impact that it expects to largely mitigate via price increases, concessions from suppliers, and shifting supply chain exposures. The company has a 12% effective tariff rate, which is lower than other retailers like The Gap (GAP), thanks to lower exposure to Asian imports. In response to tariffs, Duluth enacted July 25th and August 8th price hikes, and the impact on sales has been consistent with expectations thus far, as noted on the earnings call.

Duluth has $6 million in cash and $73 million in liquidity. Against this, it has $33 million of debt. So far this year, the company has -$28 million of free cash flow. That is primarily due to -$27 million of working capital movements so far this year, which should improve in the second half of the year. With about $17 million in cap-ex, I expect Duluth to need to generate ~$23 million in EBITDA to be free cash flow neutral, adjusting for working capital.

Given uncertainty, maintaining guidance is reasonable if slightly conservative

Alongside results and despite the beat, management is maintaining guidance for $20-25 million of EBITDA. This points to the business being roughly cash flow neutral ex-working capital this year. On net, my expectation is for working capital to improve, and I expect debt to end the year below $15 million. Given the strength in Q2, there could arguably have been an increase in guidance. However, with the turnaround still in the early stage, simply maintaining guidance is the prudent move, in my view.

I also believe being cautious about raising guidance makes sense, given two factors. First, it is still early days for the tariff-related price hikes, and it is possible that we could see a larger impact on volumes. Now, fortunately for the company, we are seeing apparel spending pick back up this year after a slow period from 2022-2024. I have been expecting apparel spending to rise 0-2% this year. This remains my view, though Q2 was much stronger. I believe some consumers pulled forward purchases to front-run tariffs. As such, some of Q2 sales may have cannibalized H2 sales, further justifying maintaining cautious H2 guidance.

DLTH stock is attractive below book value

2025 is proving to be a key inflection point, with the company’s efforts to restore margins showing signs of working. I see Duluth being cash flow neutral this year, adjusting for working capital. Now, even with their 50% rally, Duluth shares are trading below book value of about $4.80. A discount to book value is reasonable for a company consistently burning cash flow, but as Duluth moves to being cash flow positive, I see room for its share price to continue to approach book value.

A rally to book value would represent another 33% upside. Ultimately, we need to see several more quarters of improving margins to get shares back to book value, but this appears to be the trajectory to me. Ultimately, we need to see Duluth migrate towards ~$35 million in EBITDA, or another ~2% of margin accretion, to generate enough free cash flow to support a $5+ share price. This still is a sub-7% EBITDA margin, which is not particularly aggressive.

While Duluth is a speculative stock given its size, with sufficient liquidity, improving free cash flow, and a sound strategy, I view shares as attractive for investors with higher risk tolerances. Ultimately, I see shares moving towards $4.50-$4.80 over the next year, approaching book value, creating sufficient upside to justify buying shares.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Losing Tech, But Gaining The Financial Sector, Wouldn't Be So Bad
data.description:
By Mike Larson

“What if we lose tech?” I see a version of that question all the time. But if technology stocks falter... and stocks in the sector I’m going to talk about today pick up the baton... it wouldn’t be so bad. Not by a long shot!

Take a look at my first MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) going back six months. You can see that XLK has been flattening out after a long advance... that the Relative Strength Index (RSI) has been weakening... and that we could soon see a negative crossover in shorter-term moving averages (the 20-day and 50-day EMA).

Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK)

(Source: StockCharts)

All in all, that tells me tech looks tired. But tired isn’t bearish. It wouldn’t be the worst thing if tech took a breather after a huge advance... especially when you consider my second MoneyShow Chart of the Day.

This shows the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF). Unlike XLK, it recently broke out on a gap-up move. Then it retested the breakout... and the 20-day EMA... and held. All of the major moving averages are still climbing, and RSI is stair-stepping higher along with the ETF.

Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF)

(Source: StockCharts)

Financials are a cornerstone sector. Financial stocks typically falter when economic and credit concerns pile up. But they aren’t. Instead, shares of banks, brokers, insurers, and other similar companies are doing just fine - helped along by the fact the Treasury yield curve is steepening.

Bottom line? Companies in the XLF may not be as sexy as companies in the XLK. They may not get as much media attention.

But if they keep rallying - and tech stocks just mark time - I’m fine with the “trade.” If you’re a market bull, you should be, too!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Retirement Passive Income: Safer +8% Yields For Your Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

In our Wednesday article, we explored how mortgage-backed securities form the backbone of the US housing market, helping millions of families achieve the dream of homeownership. Today, we will take a closer look at corporate lending. We will discuss how this has significantly transformed over the years, evolving from a system that only catered to the big blue chip firms, to the level and scale it is today.

History of Corporate Lending in America

During the Great Depression era, most companies relied almost entirely on banks to borrow. These big banking lenders typically held every loan they originated on their balance sheets and collected interest throughout the duration of the loan. Lending was constrained by the bank’s capital limits, and during economic downturns, banks would get very conservative with their lending, resulting in rapid credit tightening. Only the largest and successful companies could access such a capital market. Small and mid-sized companies had limited and expensive options.

As you can see, this system operated on a cycle. In good times, banks were flexible with lending. However, when the economy slowed down or slipped into a recession, banks would tighten their purse strings just at the time companies desperately needed liquidity. With the absence of a bond market, loans were highly illiquid, often trading as full loans, exposing investors to significant risk. This forced banks to stay cautious and selective at the expense of borrowers and the economy.

The limitations of banks led to two complementary solutions in the 1980s. The U.S. financial system gave rise to CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations), which provided a framework to recycle existing loans to expand lending capacity. At the same time, the Small Business Investment Incentive Act led to the formation of BDCs (business development companies), which channel investor capital directly to middle-market companies. Let us examine each more closely.

Why should the economy be limited by the appetite and capacity of banks?

In the 1980s, corporate loans began to be packaged into securities that could be sold to investors. With this, banks were no longer forced to hold every loan to maturity, allowing them to recycle capital, make more loans, and transfer risk to a broader base of institutional investors.

Today, CLOs have grown into a trillion-dollar market, serving as a vital source of financing for corporate borrowers. By pooling loans, creating liquidity, and spreading risk among investors, CLOs enable banks to extend far more credit than they could on their own. Without CLOs, the corporate loan market would be significantly smaller, less efficient, and much more vulnerable to economic shocks.

CLOs are pools of loans that are divided into different tranches. CLOs are predominantly floating-rate instruments, meaning their income adjusts with changes in interest rates. The AAA tranches are paid first, making them the safest but lowest yielding. Equity tranches are paid last, so they carry the most risk but bring the potential for the highest returns. Source

CLOs, including the equity tranche, are backed by hundreds of loans, so the risk is diversified across many companies. These instruments have structural protections like overcollateralization and interest coverage tests, which redirect cash to senior tranches if collateral quality deteriorates. If there's any vulnerability in the economy, payments are directed to the higher tranches to protect the integrity of the structure. Historically, senior CLO tranches have seen virtually no defaults, making them a remarkably resilient source of income.

While the equity tranche takes the first losses, it's not exposed to any single loan default; only large cumulative losses threaten these instruments. In healthy credit environments, defaults are typically low, and equity tranches can capture most of the upside in cash flows.

We have regularly discussed two funds that invest in CLO equity tranches. There are many investors who either don’t understand them or are misinformed about or misunderstand the risks and performance against market forces. While the CLO equity class may not be something everyone is comfortable with, we will discuss securities higher up in the capital structure of these CEFs. These offer safer and steadier income. Today, we will discuss fixed-income securities from these funds that enjoy a much higher degree of safety and offer better immunity to market volatility.

OXLC Preferreds and Baby Bonds – Up To 8.5% Yields

Oxford Lane Capital Corp. (OXLC) is one of the largest CEFs that exclusively focus on CLO equity tranches. OXLC common stock plunged after its Q1 2025 earnings, which revealed large equity dilution, low NII, and management announced a 12.5% distribution cut and a reverse-split on the common stock.

What many investors missed or misunderstood was that the CEF made $441 million in investments in CLO equity debt, and that the fund has $705 million of new issue CLOs on its balance sheet that have yet to make their first cash distribution. As a result, the potential income from these investments has not yet been reflected in either the top line or bottom line. Despite this, the stock sold off sharply, driven by concerns over dilution and shrinking earnings.

CLO equity tranches take the first losses, making them sensitive to economic downturns, but they offer high yields supported by floating-rate portfolios. This tends to dissuade some investors to funds that invest in equity tranches.

Despite a rough quarter with earnings not covering their current distribution levels, OXLC preferreds and baby bonds enjoy excellent distribution coverage and present attractive ways to seek steady high yields from the CLO market.

In FY 2024, OXLC spent $38.4 million on interest expenses and $15.2 million on preferred stock dividends. After factoring in these expenses, OXLC reported $270.9 million net investment income for its year ended March 31, 2025.

Baby Bonds

  • 5.00% Notes due 2027 (OXLCZ) – Yield 5.1%
  • 8.75% Notes due 2030 (OXLCI) – Yield 8.5%
  • 6.75% Notes due 2031 (OXLCL) – Yield 7.1%
  • 7.95% Notes due 2032 (OXLCG) – Yield 7.9%

Among OXLC’s baby bonds, OXLCI and OXLCG currently trade at modest premiums to par, yet offer significantly higher yields to compensate for the premium. Neither is callable for another three to five years, with maturities in 2030 and 2032, respectively. OXLCI is an attractive choice among the CEF’s baby bonds and preferred shares.

Preferred Stock

  • 6.25% Series 2027, Cumulative Redeemable Term-Preferred Shares (OXLCP) – Yield 6.4%
  • 7.125% Series 2029, Cumulative Redeemable Term-Preferred Shares (OXLCN) – Yield 7.3%
  • 6.00% Series 2029, Cumulative Redeemable Term-Preferred Shares (OXLCO) – Yield 6.4%

While the bonds offer significantly better deals, for those who desire a monthly income, OXLC preferreds currently offer up to 7.3% yields.

ECC Preferreds and Baby Bonds – Up To 8.6% Yields

Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC) is also among the largest funds that invests primarily in the CLO equity tranches, and has a net asset value of $966 million. About 76% of ECC’s portfolio is CLO Equity. ECC’s reported metrics from Q2 show how diversified CLO instruments are. The fund’s portfolio had exposure to 1,916 obligors, with an average individual loan obligor exposure of 0.05%. During 1H 2025, ECC actively deployed $285 million of capital into CLO equity, CLO debt, and other related investments in both the primary and secondary markets. The weighted average expected yield on the CEF’s CLO equity portfolio stood at 18.8% at the end of June 2025. During 1H 2025, ECC generated $100 million in total investment income, and spent $13.8 million on interest expenses and $7.3 million on preferred stock dividends. Overall, ECC bonds and preferreds enjoy solid coverage from the fund’s operations and support distributions to the common stock after all fund expenses.

Baby Bonds

  • 6.6875% Notes due 2028 (ECCX) – Yield 6.7%
  • 5.375% Notes due 2029 (ECCV) – Yield 5.7%
  • 7.75% Notes due 2030 (ECCU) – Yield 7.7%
  • 6.75% Notes due 2031 (ECCW) – Yield 7.1%

ECC baby bonds currently offer up to 7.7% yields, and three trade at modest discounts to par.

Preferred Shares

  • 6.50% Series C, Cumulative Term-Preferred Stock due 2031 (ECCC) – Yield 7.0%
  • 6.75% Series D, Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (ECC.PR.D) – Yield 8.6%

  • 8.00% Series F, Term-Preferred Stock Due 2029 (ECCF) – Yield 7.9%

For those seeking monthly income, ECC preferreds currently offer excellent opportunities with higher yields. ECC-D is a perpetual preferred at a deeply discounted price, offering an 8.6% yield, while ECCF is terminal (callable in January 2026, and maturing January 2029), trading at a slight premium that's less than two months of dividend payments.

Who Will Serve the Middle Market Economy?

We discussed how the corporate lending framework before the 70s wasn't something smaller businesses could access. Ironically, small businesses represent 99% of all businesses in the country today, and are responsible for 55% of the employment. Even today, bank loans are largely inaccessible for middle-market companies despite being consistently profitable and having sound business models.

BDCs were created by Congress in 1980 to channel investor capital directly into small and mid-sized businesses. The primary objective was to support economic recovery during recessions since middle-market firms are vital for employment and growth. By providing an alternative source of funding beyond traditional banks, BDCs have significantly expanded access to credit and enabled thousands of businesses to expand, hire, and invest.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we regularly discuss several Blue-Chip BDCs and a few BDCs that maintain a niche focus. Today, we will discuss a few preferreds and baby bonds that offer steady income from this necessary sector.

RWAY Baby Bonds – Up To 7.9% Yields

Runway Growth Finance Corp. (RWAY) is a BDC that mostly provides first-lien, senior secured loans to growth-stage companies in sectors like technology, healthcare, and business services. Since its inception in fall 2021, the BDC has closed 89 transactions, funded $2.5 billion, and committed $3.1 billion in investments, while realizing 52 exits.

During 1H 2025, RWAY generated $70.5 million in total investment income, while spending $22 million on interest expenses. The BDC reduced its common stock dividend in light of reduced earnings earlier this year, but continues to make supplemental distributions. Its current common stock yield stands at 13.1% and remains covered by the BDC’s earnings.

  • 7.50% Notes Due 2027 (RWAYL) – Yield 7.4%

  • 8.00% Notes due 2027 (RWAYZ) – Yield 7.9%

RWAY baby bonds, which carry BBB+ ratings from Egan-Jones, trade at modest premiums to par. These present up to 7.9% yields that are well covered and provide steady income backed by a relatively smaller portfolio of early-stage borrowers.

TRIN Baby Bond – Up To 7.7% Yields

Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) was also born in 2021 and maintains a focus on lending to growth-oriented companies across tech lending, equipment financing, life sciences, asset-based lending, and sponsor finance. As of June 30, 2025, the BDC reported $2.3 billion in AUM, and has made $372 million in cumulative distributions since its IPO. The BDC maintains steady quarterly distributions for 22 quarters, with consistent issuance of supplemental distributions.

TRIN baby bonds are rated investment-grade BBB by Morningstar DBRS. During 1H 2025, the BDC spent $35 million on interest and related debt expenses, which was adequately supported by ~$135 million in total investment income.

  • 7.875% Notes Due 2029 (TRINZ) – Yield 7.7%

  • 7.875% Notes Due 2029 (TRINI) – Yield 7.7%

TRINI and TRINZ currently offer steady ~7.7% yields through their respective maturities in 2029.

CSWC Baby Bond – Up To 7.6% Yields

Capital Southwest Corp. (CSWC) is no stranger to HDO subscribers. This super-star BDC is on-track to match blue-chip status that's enjoyed by the likes of time-tested companies like Main Street Capital (MAIN). CSWC operates with a highly conservative leverage level of 0.82x, providing adequate room to scale up as the economic situation evolves. The BDC maintains its ability to provide consistent and well-covered dividends (and steady supplemental payments), backed by a portfolio of 122 borrowers with minimal credit issues.

During its first quarter, CSWC spent $15.2 million on interest expenses, while reporting $55.9 million in total investment income. The BDC has one public baby bond, rated investment-grade Baa3 by Moody’s, and trades at a notable premium to par.

  • 7.75% notes due 2028 (CSWCZ) – Yield 7.6%

CSWCZ just became callable, but offers a 7.6% yield at current prices. Considering the BDC has $100.0 million worth of maturities from its 3.375% notes due in October, and its weighted average interest on its credit facilities being around 6.8%, we don’t expect CSWC to rush into pursuing an early redemption of this baby bond at this time, providing potential legroom for steady income for a few years.

Conclusion

Lending in America has come a long way from the bank-dominated system of the mid-20th Century. CLOs and BDCs have filled a critical gap, transforming credit from the privilege of the few into an integral engine that supports thousands of businesses and, by extension, the American economy.

Are these structures perfect? No financial system ever is. Yet through diversification, structural protections, and active management, CLO-focused CEFs and BDCs provide a strong foundation that supports borrowers and investors.

For many investors, these securities often go overlooked or are misunderstood because their market prices can be volatile, or their mechanics seem complex. Their volatility is a result of their sensitivity to both interest rates and credit quality. However, beneath the complex surface lies one of the most important segments of the financial system. By understanding them, investors can access high, steady distributions, while also participating in the very heartbeat of America’s growth.

For those who prefer less volatility and better immunity to credit and interest rate climates, we have focused on securities higher up in the capital stack. With these, investors can enjoy better protection, stronger coverage, and greater resistance to the specific variables that create volatility in this necessary sector. This is the enduring power of fixed income – delivering stability, protection, and dependable cash flow that cuts through market noise and anchors a secure retirement.

data.title: American Century Strategic Allocation Funds Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For the quarter ended June 30, 2025, the Strategic Allocation Portfolios all produced strong positive returns, while all three underperformed their benchmarks.

Market Environment

The economic environment was mostly positive. Inflation moderated, and as of July 1, the Atlanta Federal Reserve’s GDPNow projected annualized real economic growth of 2.5% for the second quarter. The University of Michigan Consumer Confidence Index rebounded in June, posting a gain for the first time in six months.

Stocks went on a wild ride in the second quarter of 2025. Stocks initially fell sharply after the Liberation Day tariff announcement, only to rebound after the tariffs were postponed.

Large-cap stocks ended the quarter at an all-time high. The resumption of the artificial intelligence stock trade meant large-cap stocks outperformed small caps and growth stocks outperformed value. Meanwhile, both developed and emerging markets stocks outperformed U.S. stocks, benefiting from attractive relative valuations, interest rate cuts and more dependable earnings.

Bond prices generally rose. Interest rates were volatile amid uncertainty around the federal debt and deficits, tariffs, inflation and the economy. As a result, the longest-term bonds had negative returns, while shorter-term and higher-yielding bonds rose.

Key Contributors

Select equity holdings buoyed relative performance. Manager selection added the most value in Non-U.S. Small Cap, Quality Diversified International ETF and Non-U.S. Intrinsic Value, among others.

Key Detractors

Value-oriented holdings had negative manager selection. U.S. value managers struggled in a growth-led market. Notable detractors from relative performance included U.S. Mid Cap Value and Focused Large Cap Value ETF.

Positioning for the Future

Economic and market uncertainties highlight the importance of diversification. The bear and the bull faced off early in the second quarter, and the bull won. But the bear’s retreat doesn’t mean it was defeated. In fact, a growing convergence between hard and soft data has created plausible cases for both bullish and bearish scenarios for prudent investors to consider.

Favoring non-U.S. developed markets over the U.S. High valuations amid economic, inflation, trade and policy uncertainties make stocks outside the U.S. relatively more attractive. In addition, positive changes in relative momentum and macroeconomic data led us to favor non-U.S. developed stocks over U.S. equities.

In fixed income, focusing on valuation and quality. In terms of fixed-income sectors, our managers believe Treasury yields are rich for now and prefer mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities. By credit quality, our models prefer investment-grade over high-yield bonds because of macroeconomic uncertainty, relative valuations and narrow yield spreads. Within high yield, our fixed-income managers favor comparatively higher-quality bonds rated BB and B.

Interest rate changes are among the most significant factors affecting bond return. In a declining interest rate environment, bond prices rise and the fund may generate less income. In a rising interest rate environment, bond prices fall.

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPS): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities: MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later (to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities (maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality (credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds (maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic (daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.
