data.title: Shoe Carnival: A Great Step Forward (Upgrade)
data.description:
September 4th ended up being a really great day for shareholders of Shoe Carnival (NASDAQ:SCVL). Shares of the company closed up 20.3% after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue fell short of analysts’ expectations, the market was optimistic for a couple of reasons. The first of these related to the fact that earnings per share came in above what analysts were hoping to see. In addition to this, management revised higher its profitability guidance for 2025 in its entirety. And lastly, the firm provided an update on its plan to rebanner its stores in a manner that should create even greater shareholder value as time goes on.

Things are going quite well compared to when I last reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate in May of this year. Since then, the stock is up 36%. That's almost four times as high as the 10.3% increase that the S&P 500 experienced over the same window of time. You might think, after such a monumental amount of upside, that I would consider downgrading the company to a ‘hold’. This is especially true when you consider that earnings this year will be lower than what they were last year. However, the stock is still incredibly cheap, even with the weakness experienced this year compared to last year. And with management focused on long-term opportunities, I believe that it would actually be a great idea to upgrade it to a ‘strong buy’ right now.

A great business at a great price

For the second quarter of its 2025 fiscal year, Shoe Carnival reported revenue of $306.4 million. That's down from the $332.7 million that the company reported one year earlier. This decline was driven by two things. First and foremost, comparable sales declined by 7.5% year over year. And second, the number of locations that it has in operation dropped from 430 last year to 428 this year. It is important to note that revenue ended up coming in $8.1 million below what analysts were expecting.

Usually, this would warrant a big decline in share price as opposed to a surge. But when you look deeper, you see exactly why the market is optimistic. As I detailed in my previous article about the firm, management continues to rebrand, or rebanner if you will, its stores, changing them from the Shoe Carnival brand name to the Shoe Station brand name. Shoe Station was an innovative initiative that management started up a little while ago in order to cater to higher-income customers. And so far, this strategy of focusing on elite clientele has proven to be incredibly profitable. During the second quarter of this year, Shoe Station saw positive comparable sales. In fact, in the month of August, which was subsequent to the end of the second quarter, comparable sales under that banner ended up growing at a high-single-digit rate. This was driven by strength in both the children's category and the adult athletics space. By comparison, Shoe Carnival stores have been experiencing weakness. Management attributed this to consumers who make less than $40,000 remaining pressured in the current economic environment. But for those at higher income levels, the picture is solid.

I wish I could say that the bottom line for the company was growing year over year. But it didn't. Earnings per share contracted from $0.82 to $0.70, taking net profits down from $22.6 million to $19.2 million. The earnings per share reported by management, however, did come in $0.09 above what analysts were expecting to see. However, this year-over-year weakness was only because of major investments that the company has been making in transitioning from the Shoe Carnival to the Shoe Station banners. In fact, according to the company, the negative impact on earnings per share during the second quarter alone associated with these additional investments was $0.21. That's up from the $0.15 negative impact experienced in the first quarter of this year. It may seem odd that additional investments would negatively affect earnings per share. But according to management, this is because of, at least in part, the temporary closure of stores during the rebanner conversions.

The great thing is that, even though net profits contracted year over year, rebanner margins generated by the company improved by 2.7% year over year. But this has not yet been reflected in most cash flow metrics. Operating cash flow contracted from $23.6 million last year to $13.2 million this year. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $45.3 million to $48.6 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business dropped from $37.8 million to $33.7 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the first half of 2024. The same contributors to the weakness the company experienced during the second quarter pushed revenue down year over year. And it is clear to me that the aforementioned investments that management has been making into its restructuring activities have played a role in this weakness. In fact, the estimated impact from these investments in the first half of this year was to the tune of $11 million. Adding this back, net income would have been nearly flat year over year, even as revenue contracted by 7.7%.

Some of the most exciting news for the company came after the quarter ended. I already talked about comparable sales in August. According to management, the firm experienced accelerated results in the month of August because of the back-to-school season. They even called the acceleration signal the second. At the end of the second quarter of the year, the company ended up with no debt on its books and $91.9 million of cash and cash equivalents. But thanks to the strong showing in August, net cash had grown further to about $148 million by the end of that month. Management specified that the strong back-to-school season generated over $55 million of cash for the business during this window of time.

Thanks to these strong results, management decided to increase guidance for earnings per share for 2025. They now expect earnings per share to be between $1.70 and $2.10. The previous range saw a bottom of $1.60 per share. At the midpoint, we would be looking at net profits of about $52.2 million. But that would unfortunately represent a decline from the $73.8 million the company reported last year. But when you consider the impact that these short-term investments the company has made, the actual picture should look better on an adjusted basis. This is not to say that everything is going great. Management had previously expected revenue to come in at between $1.15 billion and $1.23 billion for the year. That compares to the $1.20 billion the company reported for 2024. But now, management expects that range to be between $1.12 billion and $1.15 billion, likely because of the weakness associated with the Shoe Carnival brand.

Management announced in the quarterly release that by the end of this year, around 145 of its locations, or 34% of the total, will have been rebranded to the Shoe Station format. This will come as 58 additional stores are rebannered in the second half of this year. And by the 2026 back-to-school season, the company expects the number of locations that fall under the Shoe Station brand to surpass 215. That would represent over 51% of all stores open at that time. Given that these locations have higher revenue and higher margins, this paves the way for growth for the rest of this year, next year, and the year after. But it doesn't come cheap. For this year, management is allocating between $45 million and $55 million toward capital investments. Between $30 million and $35 million of that will involve its rebannering efforts.

Ignoring the potential for future growth here, if we just project out results based on what we have seen so far for this year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of about $164.4 million and EBITDA of $97.5 million for this year. Using these estimates, as well as historical results that the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are incredibly low multiples, especially when it comes to the cash flows. And in the table below, I stacked Shoe Carnival up against five similar enterprises. On both a price to earnings basis and a price to operating cash flow basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, only one of the companies was cheaper than it is.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Shoe Carnival 13.6 4.3 5.7
Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT) 30.1 32.7 18.4
Skechers U.S.A. (SKX) 42.7 15.0 10.1
Torrid Holdings (CURV) 23.9 7.9 5.1
Wolverine World Wide (WWW) 31.4 17.0 20.5
Steven Madden (SHOO) 23.7 14.1 16.4

To see what kind of upside potential we might expect, I created the chart below. In it, you can see how much upside or downside shares would warrant if the firm were to trade at the same multiple as the cheapest of the five companies I compared it to. You can also see how much upside it would justify if it were to trade at the average of these firms. On the low end, we are looking at a downside of 8.8%. But in all of the other cases, we are looking at double-digit or even triple-digit potential. In the best-case scenario, we would be looking at a 303.3% surge that the company would justify. I am not predicting that will be the case. But I do think that it would not be unreasonable to expect the business to significantly outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future.

The big uncertainty here would be what will happen from a tariff perspective. But management seems optimistic, especially given current inventory levels and the high cash balance that the company has. In the firm's second-quarter earnings call, management mentioned that, based on the most recent data available, there probably will be some price increases. But those should only be between 5% and 7%. And considering that the company is moving more toward a higher-end clientele, that shouldn't be an issue for customers to absorb.

Takeaway

To be honest with you, I found the data provided by management to be very encouraging. I wish that some of the metrics had come in better for the quarter. But when you look at the picture moving forward, the company is doing really well. Management has a clear plan to take the business to the next level. And shares seem to offer meaningful upside that has the potential to significantly exceed the broader market. Given this combination of factors, I firmly believe that upgrading the company to a ‘strong buy’ would be appropriate at this time.

The Fund returned 2.16% (Institutional Class shares, net of fees, in US dollar terms) over the quarter, underperforming the index, which rose by 3.37%.1

In the industrials sector, Apple (AAPL) supply-chain companies, such as Luxshare Precision Industry, were affected by tariff-related concerns. We are monitoring developments closely and will adjust position sizing as the situation evolves and according to the outlook for each company. Meanwhile, in the consumer discretionary segment, Midea declined due to reduced trade-in subsidies, and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) fell on domestic auto-sector weakness.

Conversely, the consumer staples sector was the most significant contributor, with Yantai China Pet Foods as the top performer, as it continued to deliver strong earnings and faced limited impact from the tariffs. In the financials segment, China Construction Bank (OTCPK:CICHY), Citic Securities, Ping An Insurance (OTCPK:PNGAY), Jiangsu Changshu Rural Commercial Bank, and China Merchants Bank (OTCPK:CIHKY) performed well, given improving sentiment.

Market review

The mainland Chinese stock market rose in the second quarter of 2025. President Trump’s unexpected and severe tariff announcement in the early stages of the review period triggered market volatility, which, in turn, shifted domestic macroeconomic sentiment from AI-driven optimism to tariff-induced uncertainty. Equities subsequently recovered some lost ground as President Trump softened his stance and China signalled potential trade discussions.

The Politburo meeting in April conveyed a more supportive macroeconomic policy stance in response to US tariff hikes, focusing on preparing for external shocks, stabilising key economic areas, and integrating external and domestic trade, while reaffirming China’s commitment to opening service sectors to foreign investment.

The market rebound continued into May and June, as US-China trade tensions further de-escalated following a second round of talks in London. This development paved the way for a trade deal to be reached, whereby China would potentially relax its control over rare earth materials and open its business to more American companies in exchange for fewer US technology restrictions and easing of tariffs.

Meanwhile, the domestic economy remained on a mild recovery path. June’s export data held up well, and both the NBS and Caixin manufacturing purchasing managers’ index (PMI) surveys rose notably in June. However, concerns about a softer second-half outlook are mounting, driven by several factors. These include the payback effect from front-loaded exports, continued weakness in the property sector, and the implementation of new austerity measures. We are confident that Beijing has a comprehensive toolbox and sufficient policy reserves to achieve its target of 5.0% gross domestic product (GDP) growth.

Activity

Regarding portfolio movements, we participated in the Hong Kong initial public offering (IPO) of Chinese heating systems supplier Zhejiang Sanhua Intelligent Controls (OTC:ZSICY).

We also added Beijing New Building Materials, the world’s largest gypsum board manufacturer with a strong cost moat. The company is growing both organically and inorganically through counter-cyclical acquisitions in related sectors and is poised to become the world’s leading building materials business. The new management is young and sufficiently incentivised to deliver strong results for shareholders.

Conversely, we exited our remaining positions in China Tourism Group Duty Free, Guangzhou Baiyun Airport and Shanghai International Airport, given that these companies will likely take longer to see a meaningful recovery in the current climate. Elsewhere, we sold Proya Cosmetics and ENN Natural Gas.

Outlook

We continue to favour companies with predominantly domestic exposure and higher earnings visibility to weather further volatility. On a see-through weighted basis, approximately 80% of the revenue exposure of our A-share strategies is derived domestically, with the remainder diversified into non-US regions. We remain incrementally positive following the policy pivot late last year, and external pressure may prompt a greater focus on domestic stimulus, which is key to turning the economy around.
